mn109:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in the Eastern Monastery, the palace of Migāra’s mother.
mn109:2.1: And on that occasion—the uposatha of the fifteenth, the night of a very full moon—he was sitting out in the open with the Saṅgha of monks.
mn109:3.1: Then a certain monk, rising from his seat, arranging his robe over one shoulder, and placing his hands palm-to-palm over the heart, said to the Blessed One:
mn109:3.2: “Venerable sir, there is an area where, if the Blessed One would give me leave, I would like the answer to a question.”
mn109:3.3: “Very well, then, monk. Sit back down in your seat and ask whatever you want.”
mn109:3.4: Responding to the Blessed One, “Yes, lord,” the monk sat back down in his seat and said to the Blessed One,
mn109:4.2: the form clinging-aggregate, the feeling clinging-aggregate, the perception clinging-aggregate, the fabrications clinging-aggregate, the consciousness clinging-aggregate.”
mn109:4.3: “Monk, these are the five clinging-aggregates, i.e.,
mn109:4.4: the form clinging-aggregate, the feeling clinging-aggregate, the perception clinging-aggregate, the fabrications clinging-aggregate, the consciousness clinging-aggregate.”
mn109:4.5: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk delighted & approved of the Blessed One’s words and then asked him a further question:
mn109:5.1: “But in what, lord, are these five clinging-aggregates rooted?”
mn109:5.2: “Monk, these five clinging-aggregates are rooted in desire.”
mn109:6.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “Is clinging the same thing as the five clinging-aggregates, or is clinging separate from the five clinging-aggregates?”
mn109:6.2: “Monk, clinging is neither the same thing as the five clinging-aggregates, nor is it separate from the five clinging-aggregates.
mn109:6.3: Just that whatever passion & delight is there, that’s the clinging there.”
mn109:7.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “Might there be diversity in the desire & passion for the five clinging-aggregates?”
mn109:7.3: There is the case where the thought occurs to someone,
mn109:7.4: ‘May I be one with such a form in the future. May I be one with such a feeling… perception… fabrications… such a consciousness in the future.
mn109:7.5: This is how there would be diversity in the desire & passion for the five clinging-aggregates.”
mn109:8.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “To what extent does the designation ‘aggregate’ apply to the aggregates?”
mn109:8.2: “Monk, whatever form is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: That is called the form aggregate.
mn109:8.3: Whatever feeling is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: That is called the feeling aggregate.
mn109:8.4: Whatever perception is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: That is called the perception aggregate.
mn109:8.5: Whatever fabrications are past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: Those are called the fabrications aggregate.
mn109:8.6: Whatever consciousness is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: That is called the consciousness aggregate.
mn109:8.7: This is the extent to which the term ‘aggregate’ applies to the aggregates.”
mn109:9.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “Lord, what is the cause, what the condition, for the delineation of the form aggregate?
mn109:9.2: What is the cause, what the condition, for the delineation of the feeling aggregate…
mn109:9.6: “Monk, the four great elements [earth, water, fire, & wind] are the cause, the four great elements the condition, for the delineation of the form aggregate.
mn109:9.7: Contact is the cause, contact the condition, for the delineation of the feeling aggregate.
mn109:9.8: Contact is the cause, contact the condition, for the delineation of the perception aggregate.
mn109:9.9: Contact is the cause, contact the condition, for the delineation of the fabrications aggregate.
mn109:9.10: Name-&-form is the cause, name-&-form the condition, for the delineation of the consciousness aggregate.”
mn109:10.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “Lord, how does self-identification view come about?”
mn109:10.2: “There is the case, monk, where an uninstructed run-of-the-mill person—who has no regard for noble ones, is not well-versed or disciplined in their Dhamma; who has no regard for people of integrity, is not well-versed or disciplined in their Dhamma—
mn109:10.3: assumes form to be the self, or the self as possessing form, or form as in the self, or the self as in form.
mn109:10.4: He assumes feeling to be the self, or the self as possessing feeling, or feeling as in the self, or the self as in feeling.
mn109:10.5: He assumes perception to be the self, or the self as possessing perception, or perception as in the self, or the self as in perception.
mn109:10.6: He assumes fabrications to be the self, or the self as possessing fabrications, or fabrications as in the self, or the self as in fabrications.
mn109:10.7: He assumes consciousness to be the self, or the self as possessing consciousness, or consciousness as in the self, or the self as in consciousness.
mn109:10.8: “This, monk, is how self-identification view comes about.”
mn109:11.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “Lord, how does self-identification view no longer come about?”
mn109:11.2: “There is the case, monk, where a well-instructed disciple of the noble ones—who has regard for noble ones, is well-versed & disciplined in their Dhamma; who has regard for people of integrity, is well-versed & disciplined in their Dhamma—
mn109:11.3: doesn’t assume form to be the self, or the self as possessing form, or form as in the self, or the self as in form.
mn109:11.4: He doesn’t assume feeling to be the self.…
mn109:11.5: doesn’t assume perception to be the self.…
mn109:11.6: doesn’t assume fabrications to be the self.…
mn109:11.7: He doesn’t assume consciousness to be the self, or the self as possessing consciousness, or consciousness as in the self, or the self as in consciousness.
mn109:11.8: “This, monk, is how self-identification view no longer comes about.”
mn109:12.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “What, lord, is the allure of form? What is its drawback? What is the escape from it?
mn109:12.2: What is the allure of feeling…
mn109:12.5: consciousness? What is its drawback? What is the escape from it?”
mn109:12.6: “Monk, whatever pleasure & joy arises dependent on form: That is the allure of form.
mn109:12.7: The fact that form is inconstant, stressful, subject to change: That is the drawback of form.
mn109:12.8: The subduing of desire & passion, the abandoning of desire & passion for form: That is the escape from form.
mn109:12.9: “Whatever pleasure & joy arises dependent on feeling: That is the allure of feeling.…
mn109:12.10: “Whatever pleasure & joy arises dependent on perception: That is the allure of perception.…
mn109:12.11: “Whatever pleasure & joy arises dependent on fabrications: That is the allure of fabrications.…
mn109:12.12: “Whatever pleasure & joy arises dependent on consciousness: That is the allure of consciousness.
mn109:12.13: The fact that consciousness is inconstant, stressful, subject to change: That is the drawback of consciousness.
mn109:12.14: The subduing of desire & passion, the abandoning of desire & passion for consciousness: That is the escape from consciousness.”
mn109:13.1: Saying, “Very good, lord,” the monk… asked him a further question: “Knowing in what way, seeing in what way, is there—with regard to this body endowed with consciousness, and with regard to all external signs—no longer any I-making, or my-making, or obsession with conceit?”
mn109:13.2: “Monk, one sees any form whatsoever—past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near—every form, as it has come to be with right discernment: ‘This is not mine. This is not my self. This is not what I am.’
mn109:13.6: “One sees any consciousness whatsoever—past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near—every consciousness—as it has come to be with right discernment: ‘This is not mine. This is not my self. This is not what I am.’”
mn109:13.7: “Monk, knowing in this way, seeing in this way is there—with regard to this body endowed with consciousness, and with regard to all external signs—no longer any I-making, or my-making, or obsession with conceit.”
mn109:14.1: Now at that moment this line of thinking appeared in the awareness of a certain monk:
mn109:14.2: “So—form is not-self, feeling is not-self, perception is not-self, fabrications are not-self, consciousness is not-self.
mn109:14.3: Then what self will be touched by the actions done by what is not-self?”
mn109:14.4: Then the Blessed One, realizing with his awareness the line of thinking in that monk’s awareness, addressed the monks:
mn109:14.5: “It’s possible that a senseless person—immersed in ignorance, overcome with craving—might think that he could outsmart the Teacher’s message in this way:
mn109:14.6: ‘So—form is not-self, feeling is not-self, perception is not-self, fabrications are not-self, consciousness is not-self.
mn109:14.7: Then what self will be touched by the actions done by what is not-self?’
mn109:14.8: Now, monks, haven’t I trained you in counter-questioning with regard to this & that topic here & there?
mn109:15.4: “And is that which is inconstant easeful or stressful?”—
mn109:15.6: “And is it fitting to regard what is inconstant, stressful, subject to change as:
mn109:15.7: ‘This is mine. This is my self. This is what I am’?”
mn109:16.2: Is feeling constant or inconstant?”—“Inconstant, lord.” …
mn109:16.3: “… Is perception constant or inconstant?”—“Inconstant, lord.” …
mn109:16.4: “… Are fabrications constant or inconstant?”—“Inconstant, lord.” …
mn109:16.5: “What do you think, monks? Is consciousness constant or inconstant?”—
mn109:16.7: “And is that which is inconstant easeful or stressful?”—
mn109:16.9: “And is it fitting to regard what is inconstant, stressful, subject to change as:
mn109:16.10: ‘This is mine. This is my self. This is what I am’?”
mn109:16.12: “Thus, monks, any form whatsoever that is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: Every form is to be seen as it has come to be with right discernment as: ‘This is not mine. This is not my self. This is not what I am.’
mn109:17.4: “Any consciousness whatsoever that is past, future, or present; internal or external; blatant or subtle; common or sublime; far or near: Every consciousness is to be seen as it has come to be with right discernment as: ‘This is not mine. This is not my self. This is not what I am.’
mn109:17.5: “Seeing thus, the instructed disciple of the noble ones grows disenchanted with form, disenchanted with feeling, disenchanted with perception, disenchanted with fabrications, disenchanted with consciousness.
mn109:18.1: Disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion, he is released. With release, there is the knowledge, ‘Released.’
mn109:18.2: He discerns that ‘Birth is ended, the holy life fulfilled, the task done. There is nothing further for this world.’”
mn109:18.3: That is what the Blessed One said.
mn109:18.4: Gratified, the monks delighted in the Blessed One’s words.
mn109:18.5: And while this explanation was being given, the minds of sixty-some monks, through lack of clinging/sustenance, were released from effluents.
